activities_projects.title: Genealogical Politics: Timurid vs. Mongol
activities_projects.objective: Deconstruct the Mughal obsession with lineage and legitimacy
steps: Analyze why the Mughals violently rejected the 'Mongol' label despite their maternal descent from Genghis Khan
Explain the political significance of Timur's 1398 invasion of Delhi in justifying Mughal claims to the throne
Examine the 'Timurid Group Portrait' and explain how pictorial representation served as a tool for dynastic legitimacy
Critique the shift in nomenclature from 'Mughal' (an outsider's term) to their self-identification as 'Gurkani'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates how ancestral heritage and historical memory are manipulated to build imperial authority
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical depth in analyzing the preference for the Timurid legacy over the Mongol past (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Mansabdari-Jagirdari Crisis: A Structural Failure
activities_projects.objective: Investigate the economic implosion of the late Mughal state
steps: Compare the fiscal stability of the 'Jagirs' under Akbar (where 'Jama' matched 'Hasil') versus the inflation under Aurangzeb
Analyze the 'Be-jagiri' (landlessness) crisis and how it forced Mansabdars to over-exploit the peasantry
Trace the link between the surge in 'Zat' ranks and the corresponding decrease in the quality of the 'Sawar' (cavalry)
Write a thesis on how the system of 'transferable land grants' inadvertently discouraged long-term agricultural investment
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes administrative data to explain the structural decay and the eventual collapse of central authority
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in linking bureaucratic expansion to the agrarian crisis and peasant rebellions (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Todar Mal’s Zabt: An Econometric Critique
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the sophistication and limitations of Mughal land revenue systems
steps: Deconstruct the 'Dahshala' system: How the 10-year average of prices and yields created a rigid tax structure
Evaluate the socio-economic impact of requiring revenue in 'cash' rather than 'kind'
Analyze the spatial limitations of Zabt—why it failed in peripheral zones like Bengal and Gujarat
Discuss the role of the 'Patwari' and 'Qanungo' as the state's eyes in the rural hinterland
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the scientific rigor of Mughal bureaucracy and its impact on rural class stratification
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical accuracy in explaining the 10-year revenue calculation and its regional variations (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Ibadat Khana: From Orthodoxy to Sulh-i Kul
activities_projects.objective: Trace the evolution of Akbar’s religious and political philosophy
materials: Excerpts from Abul Fazl and Badauni
steps: Analyze the shift from the sectarian debates of the 'Ibadat Khana' to the pluralistic framework of 'Sulh-i Kul'
Contrast the viewpoints of the 'Ulama' with Akbar’s vision of 'Universal Peace'
Identify how 'Sulh-i Kul' functioned as a political necessity for an empire with a Hindu majority and a Muslim ruling elite
Research the 'Mahzar' (Infallibility Decree) of 1579 and its role in centralizing religious authority in the Emperor
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the transition of the Mughal state from a theological entity to a secular-bureaucratic one
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to connect religious policy with the practicalities of governing a diverse subcontinent (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Nur Jahan and the ‘Junta’ Theory
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the mechanisms of female political agency in an absolute monarchy
steps: Critically examine the 'Junta' theory: Did Nur Jahan lead a faction that controlled Jahangir, or was she a co-sovereign?
Analyze the symbolic weight of the 'Sikka' (coinage) and 'Khutba' (sermon) in the name of a female consort
Investigate her role in architectural innovation (e.g., Itimad-ud-Daulah’s tomb) as a form of cultural patronage
Compare her authority with previous queens to assess the uniqueness of her political position
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the extent and limits of gendered power within the high-Mughal court
activities_projects.evaluation: Interpretation of coins and farmans as primary evidence of shared sovereignty (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Sisodiya Paradox: Diplomacy vs. Conquest
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the Mughal strategy of 'honorable submission'
steps: Examine why the Sisodiyas of Mewar resisted the Mughals longer than any other Rajput house
Analyze the 'Watan Jagir' concept: How returning conquered land as a gift ensured loyalty
Contrast the treatment of the Ranas of Mewar with Aurangzeb’s later treatment of Maratha leaders like Shivaji
Evaluate the long-term impact of the Rajput-Mughal matrimonial and military alliance on Indian statecraft
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains the success of the Mughal Empire as a 'consensual' state supported by local elites
activities_projects.evaluation: Strength of the argument regarding the diplomatic vs. military cost of empire-building (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Coparcenary Succession: The Catalyst for Civil War
activities_projects.objective: Investigate the institutional instability caused by Timurid inheritance laws
materials: Case study of the 1657-1658 war of succession
steps: Differentiate between the European 'Primogeniture' and the Timurid 'Coparcenary' (division of inheritance)
Analyze why the lack of a clear law of succession led to the 'War of the Princes' in every generation
Evaluate the ideological conflict between Dara Shukoh (liberalism) and Aurangzeb (orthodoxy)
Propose an alternative succession model that could have saved the empire from internal depletion
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the structural flaw in the Mughal legal system that led to periodic political paralysis
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical analysis of the civil wars and their impact on the treasury and nobility (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Economic Paradox: Opulence vs. Famine
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the wealth distribution in the 17th-century Mughal economy
steps: Research the statistic that 445 nobles controlled 61.5% of the total revenue under Shah Jahan
Contrast the 'Great Mughal' image presented in Europe with the reality of rural 'hand-to-mouth' existence
Analyze why the secondary producers (artisans/peasants) were unable to invest in technological upgrades
Evaluate the role of the 'Zamindar' as both a protector of the peasant and a rebel against the state
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically assesses the extractive nature of the Mughal state and its impact on economic development
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of analysis on the 'Gilded Age' of the Mughals and the underlying poverty (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Abul Fazl vs. Badauni: A Historiographical Duel
activities_projects.objective: Compare official court history with secret dissenting accounts
materials: Excerpts from Akbar Nama and Muntakhab-ut-Tawarikh
steps: Analyze Abul Fazl’s portrayal of Akbar as a 'divinely guided' philosopher-king
Contrast this with Abdul Qadir Badauni’s secret critique of Akbar’s religious innovations
Explain how the 'Ain-i Akbari' served as a data-driven justification for centralized rule
Discuss the ethics of historical writing: Which account is more 'reliable' for a modern historian?
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the existence of multiple, conflicting narratives within the same historical period
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical comparison of the two authors' perspectives and their impact on historical memory (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 17th Century and the Rise of Successor States
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the fragmentation of the empire into regional powers
steps: Investigate how Aurangzeb’s long campaigns in the Deccan drained the northern administration
Analyze the rise of powerful governors (Nizams, Nawabs) who acknowledged the Mughal Emperor only in name
Explain the concept of 'Fragmented Sovereignty'—where the Mughal prestige survived but their power vanished
Compare the administrative continuity in 'Successor States' like Awadh and Hyderabad with the Mughal model
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the transition from a centralized empire to a system of regional states
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in explaining the process of decentralization and the survival of the Mughal 'myth' (10 marks)
